claim: Nine candidate data sources for birth-registration analysis have been classified into usable, limited, and rejected categories with explicit reasons covering measure family, timing, geographic grain, and health-linkage relevance, separating survey-based under-5 registration, certificate possession, administrative CRVS completeness, and health-touchpoint data.
failure_modes: A reader treats the ID4D 850 million legal-identity figure as equivalent to UNICEF 200 million under-5 birth-registration figure, when the former includes adults and uses a different methodology.
A reader treats UNSD self-reported CRVS completeness as independently verified, when the portal aggregates country self-reports without auditing.
A reader uses the source inventory as a ranking of countries by registration gap, when the sources are classified by usability for analysis, not by country performance.
A reader treats DHS and MICS as interchangeable, when their questionnaire customizations, roster practices, and country coverage differ.
A reader treats the WHO-UNICEF 2021 guidance as proof that health-sector linkage already works in a specific country, when it is operational guidance not an outcomes evaluation.
kill_condition: A reviewer identifies a candidate source that should have been classified but was omitted, or demonstrates that an existing classification reason is factually wrong about the source's measure family, grain, or access conditions.
limitations: The source inventory covers global and regional sources but does not yet include country-specific CRVS documentation for any individual country.
Health-touchpoint sources are classified as limited because their access conditions are country-specific and could not be independently verified at the inventory stage.
The inventory does not assess data quality within any single country's survey or administrative system.
